questions.question: In Depth First Search (DFS), what does 'Depth' refer to?
options: The order in which vertices are visited
The distance from the starting vertex to each vertex
The amount of memory used by the algorithm
The time complexity of the algorithm
questions.explanation: In DFS, 'depth' refers to the distance from the starting vertex to each vertex.

questions.question: What is the main difference between DFS and Breadth First Search (BFS)?
options: DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking
BFS explores all vertices at a given depth before moving to the next depth level
questions.explanation: DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, whereas BFS explores all vertices at a given depth before moving to the next depth level.

questions.question: Which data structure is typically used to implement the recursion stack in DFS?
questions.explanation: A stack is typically used to implement the recursion stack in DFS.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of a basic Depth First Search algorithm?
questions.explanation: A basic Depth First Search algorithm has a time complexity of O(n), where n is the number of vertices in the graph.

questions.question: How often does DFS visit all vertices in a graph?
options: Only if the graph is connected
Only if the graph is acyclic
questions.explanation: DFS visits all vertices in a graph if and only if the graph is connected and acyclic.
